skill.name: result-figure-consistencycheck
skill.description: Checks consistency between paper result descriptions and figure legends (text-only) when the input is a PDF-to-Markdown full text containing page breaks (e.g., `## Page XX`) and legend text; outputs a Markdown consistency report and a UTF-8 CSV issue list.
skill.summary: Compares result descriptions against figure legends in PDF-to-Markdown text and generates a structured consistency report with discrepancy tracking.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 45 static analysis findings are false positives. The 21 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts stem from markdown '##' header syntax being misidentified as cryptographic hash patterns. The 22 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts are markdown inline code formatting backticks, not shell commands. The 4 'System reconnaissance' alerts are legacy audit notes in a JSON file. The only true finding is a hardcoded GitHub URL in SKILL.md:7, which is a legitimate source attribution link with no exfiltration risk. This is a text-based academic consistency checking tool with no malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in documentation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded GitHub URL pointing to the source repository. This is a legitimate source attribution link for the skill and poses no data exfiltration risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown header syntax misidentified as cryptographic algorithm
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged markdown '##' header syntax and '## Page XX' example text as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' patterns. These are standard markdown headings and example formatting in a text-only consistency checking skill. No cryptographic operations exist in this skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown code backticks misidentified as shell execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged markdown inline code backticks as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All instances are markdown formatting for file paths, example text, and code blocks. No actual shell command execution occurs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Audit JSON notes misidentified as system reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged legacy audit evaluation notes in result-figure-consistencycheck_audit_result_v1.json as 'System reconnaissance'. These are plain text notes from a previous evaluation describing assertion results. No reconnaissance or system scanning commands exist in this file.

content.user_title: Check Figure Legend Consistency in Research Papers
content.value_statement: When editing academic papers, matching result descriptions with figure legends is time-consuming and error-prone. This skill automatically compares text from PDF-to-Markdown output and lists all discrepancies in a structured report.
actual_capabilities: Compare result descriptions against figure legends using full text from PDF-to-Markdown conversion
Detect missing figure references in the Results section of academic papers
Identify numerical and label mismatches between legends and Results paragraphs
Generate a Markdown consistency report following a structured template
Produce a UTF-8 CSV issue list with figure number, location, description, and priority
Record discrepancies at the panel or sub-figure level for precise tracking
limitations: Does not read or analyze figure images or visual content of any kind
Requires pre-processed PDF-to-Markdown text with page break markers as input
Consistency check is limited to text comparison between legends and Results sections only
Cannot verify numerical accuracy or statistical validity of reported data

use_cases.title: Academic Manuscript Revision
use_cases.description: A researcher preparing a journal revision uploads the full text PDF converted to Markdown. The skill identifies figure legends that describe analyses not mentioned in the Results section.

use_cases.title: Editorial Quality Control
use_cases.description: A journal editor checks a submitted manuscript for consistency between figure captions and result descriptions before sending for peer review.

use_cases.title: Collaborative Paper Review
use_cases.description: A research team compares multiple versions of a paper draft to ensure figure references stay consistent as the manuscript evolves through revisions.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Consistency Check
prompt_templates.prompt: I have converted a research paper PDF to Markdown. Please check if the figure legends match the result descriptions in the text.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user wanting a quick overview of discrepancies

prompt_templates.title: Targeted Panel-Level Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Please check Figure 3 in detail. Compare each panel (A, B, C) legend description with the corresponding Results section text and list any mismatches at the panel level.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs detailed analysis of a specific figure

prompt_templates.title: Full Report Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a complete consistency check on this paper. Generate a Markdown report using the template in assets/consistency_template.md and a CSV issue list with all discrepancies found.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user preparing a comprehensive audit for manuscript revision

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Version Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I have two versions of the same paper Markdown (v1 and v2). Check figure consistency in each version separately, then compare the results to see which discrepancies were resolved and which remain.
prompt_templates.scenario: Power user tracking changes across manuscript revisions

output_examples.input: Check consistency between Results text and figure legends in the paper Markdown.
output_examples.output: The skill identified 3 discrepancies: Figure 2B mentions n=12 but Results text reports n=10. Figure 4A describes a time point at 72 hours while legend says 48 hours. Figure 5 panel C analysis is not referenced anywhere in the Results section.

output_examples.input: Please check Figure 1 and 2 for panel-level mismatches.
output_examples.output: Figure 1: Panels A and B are consistent. Panel C legend mentions p-value less than 0.01 but Results reports p-value less than 0.05. Figure 2: Panel A is missing from the Results text entirely. Panel B terminology mismatch: legend says treatment group but text says intervention group.

output_examples.input: Generate a full consistency report for this manuscript.
output_examples.output: Report generated: consistency_report.md with 4 issues found. CSV file saved with columns: Figure Number, Location/Reference, Issue Description, Suggested Revision, Priority. Top priority issue: Figure 3 legend describes survival analysis not present in any Results paragraph.

best_practices: Convert the PDF to Markdown using a reliable tool that preserves page break markers for accurate comparison
Review the CSV output first to get a quick overview of all issues, then read the detailed Markdown report for context
Verify each flagged discrepancy manually before making changes to the manuscript to avoid introducing errors
anti_patterns: Do not assume the skill checks image content - it only compares text descriptions from the Markdown input
Do not run the check on raw PDF files without converting to Markdown with page break markers first
Do not ignore minor terminology mismatches as they may indicate deeper inconsistencies in the paper

faq.question: Does this skill read images in the paper?
faq.answer: No. It compares only text from the figure legends and Results sections as they appear in the Markdown input.

faq.question: What input format does the skill require?
faq.answer: It requires a PDF-to-Markdown conversion of the full paper text that includes page break markers.

faq.question: What outputs does the skill produce?
faq.answer: It generates a Markdown consistency report and a UTF-8 CSV issue list with structured fields for tracking.

faq.question: Can the skill detect numerical errors in the data?
faq.answer: No. It can flag numerical mismatches between legends and text but cannot verify the actual statistical accuracy.

faq.question: What language are the outputs in?
faq.answer: The default output language is Chinese. You can specify another language if needed.

faq.question: Can this skill be used for peer review?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps reviewers verify that figure references and descriptions are consistent throughout the manuscript.
